questions.text: In the beginning of the chapter, a singer is seen singing which famous song?
questions.explanation: Lata Mangeshkar is depicted singing this song in memory of the martyrs.

questions.text: What was the main reason the British East India Company could extend its power in India during the 18th century?
options: Indian princes were short-sighted and fought each other
questions.explanation: Internal rivalry among princes and better British arms allowed the company to take control.

questions.text: Who was the far-sighted ruler of Mysore who fought the British until he died fighting?
questions.explanation: Tipu Sultan is remembered as a brave king who resisted British expansion.

questions.text: How did many Indians initially react to British conquest according to the 'Glimpses'?
options: Some thanked the British for bringing peace and ending raids
questions.explanation: Some believed the British brought stability and ended the era of 'thugs'.

questions.text: Which social evils were preached by religious leaders during the British era?
questions.explanation: Religious figures often promoted regressive practices during this time.

questions.text: Who was Raja Ram Mohan Roy?
options: A learned man from Bengal and a social reformer
A religious leader who supported Sati
questions.explanation: He was a pioneer of social reform who understood the importance of modern knowledge.

questions.text: What did Raja Ram Mohan Roy tell his wife, Uma, to explain the unity of religions?
options: Cows are of different colors, but the color of their milk is the same
We should follow only one god
Kings are more important than priests
questions.explanation: He used the analogy to show that while teachers have different opinions, the essence of every religion is the same.

questions.text: Why did Raja Ram Mohan Roy go to England?
options: To see what made the British powerful and remind them of their duty to subjects
questions.explanation: He wanted to understand British power and represent Indian interests.

questions.text: What was the 'Regulation III' passed by the British in 1818?
options: An Indian could be jailed without trial in court
questions.explanation: This was a tool of oppression used to suppress Indian voices without legal process.

questions.text: What did the British do to Indian industries?
options: They imposed heavy taxes on Indian goods to favor British imports
questions.explanation: British policies led to the ruin of traditional Indian handicrafts and industries.

questions.text: In 1835, what did Lord Macaulay suggest for Indian education?
questions.explanation: He wanted to produce a class of clerks to help the British administration.

questions.text: What was the unintended effect of English education on Indians?
options: It produced a new generation of intellectuals who wanted to change society
questions.explanation: Educated Indians began to discuss reforms and national unity.

questions.text: By 1856, the British had conquered ________ of India.
questions.explanation: The map in the chapter shows British territory covering almost all of India by 1856.

questions.text: Who were the 'Santhals'?
options: A tribal group who revolted after losing their lands in Bengal
questions.explanation: They rose in rebellion in 1855 and killed Europeans and their servants.

questions.text: Who was Mangal Pandey?
options: A sepoy who attacked the adjutant of his regiment in Barrackpore
questions.explanation: He is one of the most famous early heroes of the 1857 revolt.

questions.text: What rumor spread among the sepoys about the new cartridges?
options: They were greased with the fat of cows and pigs
questions.explanation: This hurt the religious sentiments of both Hindu and Muslim soldiers.

questions.text: How did the message of revolt spread among the villagers?
questions.explanation: Chapatis were passed from village to village to signal readiness for the emperor's support.

questions.text: What was used to spread the message among Indian soldiers (sepoys)?
questions.explanation: Lotus flowers were circulated among the Indian soldiers.

questions.text: Who was the last Mughal Emperor who led the revolt in Delhi?
questions.explanation: The rebels proclaimed him as their leader in Delhi.

questions.text: Who was the 'Begum of Lucknow' who joined the revolt because her kingdom was taken?
questions.explanation: She was one of the prominent women leaders of the 1857 uprising.

questions.text: Which Maulvi from Faizabad told people to 'Rise, brothers, rise'?
questions.explanation: He prophesied that the 'Angrez' (British) rule would soon end.

questions.text: Who was the leader in Kanpur during the revolt?
questions.explanation: Nana Sahib led the rebel forces in Kanpur.

questions.text: Who was Azimullah Khan?
questions.explanation: He played a key role in the planning of the uprising.

questions.text: Who was the 80-year-old leader from Bihar who received a bullet in his wrist?
questions.explanation: Kunwar Singh famously cut off his own hand and offered it to the River Ganga.

questions.text: What happened to Mangal Pandey for his revolt?
questions.explanation: He was hanged for his defiance, which sparked further revolt.

questions.text: The British treated Indians as 'unworthy of trust' and 'incapable of honesty'. This was stated by:
questions.explanation: This reflected the racist attitude of the colonial rulers.

questions.text: What was the main outcome of the ruin of Indian artisans?
options: Famines followed, and millions died of starvation
questions.explanation: Economic exploitation led to devastating famines in India.

questions.text: Which year is associated with the peak of the First War of Independence in this chapter?
questions.explanation: 1857 marked the start of the massive nationwide revolt.

questions.text: The British company's loot led to the end of ________.
questions.explanation: Colonial greed affected every section of Indian society.

questions.text: What does 'Glimpses of the Past' use to tell the story?
questions.explanation: The chapter is presented in a comic-strip style to make history engaging.

questions.text: Who said 'All misery in the world is due to women'?
options: A religious leader of that time
questions.explanation: Some religious leaders used such claims to justify the oppression of women.

questions.text: The British wanted to create 'Indians in blood and color, but ________ in taste, in opinions, in morals and in intellect.'
questions.explanation: This was the goal of Macaulay's education system.

questions.text: What was the destination of the sepoys after they revolted in Meerut?
questions.explanation: They marched to Delhi to meet Bahadur Shah Zafar.

questions.text: Tantia Tope was a famous leader associated with:
questions.explanation: He was a brilliant general who fought alongside Nana Sahib.

questions.text: Raja Ram Mohan Roy started a newspaper which the British ________.
questions.explanation: The British were suspicious of his influence and stopped his publications.

questions.text: What happened to the Indian princes who supported the British?
options: They became 'puppets' in the hands of the British
questions.explanation: The British used them to control the population and then gradually took over their lands.

questions.text: Why did the white soldiers get much more pay and servants than the Indian sepoys?
options: Racial discrimination by the British administration
questions.explanation: Unequal treatment was a major grievance for the Indian soldiers.

questions.text: Which city is mentioned as the 'City of the Emperor' in the context of the revolt?
questions.explanation: Delhi, being the Mughal capital, was the symbolic center.

questions.text: Who is the author/compiler of this pictorial history?
questions.explanation: The chapter is based on the work of S.D. Sawant.

questions.text: The main message of 'Glimpses of the Past' is:
options: The seeds of the 1857 revolt were sown by years of British oppression
questions.explanation: It shows how different events led up to the massive explosion of 1857.
